base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2405.16466v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2405.16466v1","title":"High-Performance Temporal Reversible Spiking Neural Networks with $O(L)$
  Training Memory and $O(1)$ Inference Cost","authors":["JiaKui Hu","Man Yao","Xuerui Qiu","Yuhong Chou","Yuxuan Cai","Ning Qiao","Yonghong Tian","Bo XU","Guoqi Li"],"published_date":"2024-05-26T07:26:56Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Multi-timestep simulation of brain-inspired Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs)
boost memory requirements during training and increase inference energy cost.
Current training methods cannot simultaneously solve both training and
inference dilemmas. This work proposes a novel Temporal Reversible architecture
for SNNs (T-RevSNN) to jointly address the training and inference challenges by
altering the forward propagation of SNNs. We turn off the temporal dynamics of
most spiking neurons and design multi-level temporal reversible interactions at
temporal turn-on spiking neurons, resulting in a $O(L)$ training memory.
Combined with the temporal reversible nature, we redesign the input encoding
and network organization of SNNs to achieve $O(1)$ inference energy cost. Then,
we finely adjust the internal units and residual connections of the basic SNN
block to ensure the effectiveness of sparse temporal information interaction.
T-RevSNN achieves excellent accuracy on ImageNet, while the memory efficiency,
training time acceleration, and inference energy efficiency can be
significantly improved by $8.6 \times$, $2.0 \times$, and $1.6 \times$,
respectively. This work is expected to break the technical bottleneck of
significantly increasing memory cost and training time for large-scale SNNs
while maintaining high performance and low inference energy cost. Source code
and models are available at: https://github.com/BICLab/T-RevSNN.","github_url":"https://github.com/BICLab/T-RevSNN","main_contributions":"The paper introduces Temporal Reversible Spiking Neural Networks (T-RevSNN) to address the high memory requirements during SNN training and high inference energy costs. T-RevSNN achieves this by turning off temporal dynamics for most spiking neurons, using multi-level temporal reversible interactions, and redesigning input encoding and network organization. This approach significantly improves memory efficiency, training time, and inference energy efficiency while maintaining competitive accuracy on ImageNet.","methodology":"T-RevSNN employs a novel architecture where temporal dynamics are selectively enabled in key spiking neurons, coupled with multi-level temporal reversible interactions. Input encoding is redesigned to divide input features into groups for each timestep. The SNN blocks are redesigned based on ConvNeXt, with adjustments to residual connections. The reversibility concept is used to reduce memory requirements by reconstructing previous states instead of storing all intermediate activations.","experimental_setup":"The proposed T-RevSNN is evaluated on ImageNet-1k for static image classification and CIFAR10-DVS and DVS128 Gesture datasets for neuromorphic classification. The models are trained and tested on NVIDIA-A100-40GB devices. Standard data augmentation techniques are used during training. Performance is measured in terms of accuracy, training time, memory usage, and inference energy.","limitations":"The paper acknowledges a performance gap compared to Transformer-based SNNs, attributing it to the architectural differences between CNN-based and Transformer-based models. The network may not converge when replacing the spiking CNN block with spiking Transformer. The paper primarily focuses on direct training methods.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include exploring Transformer-based T-RevSNN architectures, scaling the models to larger datasets and tasks, and applying T-RevSNN to more vision or long sequence tasks. This involves addressing the performance gap with Transformer-based architectures and further optimizing the network for different applications."}
